speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll do the same thing, you know, approximately when it was taken, left to right, top row, bottom row. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1947 in a city called Grosswardein. It was a year after I went home to my hometown, to Sighet. And on my way back, I stopped off and see my sisters and that's when they gave me this picture. The middle one, as you see-- the middle two are my two sisters. They married their husbands, two brothers. And their husband is behind their back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is-- Idele married Mordecai and Yehudit married Chaim. And on the left-hand side is his brother-in-law, and my brother-in-law's sister is below him. And the three little children, as you see below, is my oldest sister's three kids. They were hiding in the mountains. And they were babies actually when they were running from hiding place to hiding place, and all these three survived. The-- the little girl on the left-hand side, she is married, and she has nine children of her own. And she has four married children, and has 11 grandchildren. And she still have a houseful of children home. And if you look at her, she looks like a baby herself. And the little boy, as you see, is holding my-- my-- my sister's pocketbook. His name is Sruli Waldman. He has married the most gorgeous girl in Montreal, has two children of his own. And those two children are married, and he has six grandchildren. And the one in the corner, with a little cap on his head, with the short pants, his name is Simcha Waldman. He married an Israeli girl, and he has three children, two daughters and a son. They're all married. And he was the only grandson that my father and mother lived to see before the war. Because my sister married before the-- before the war. And so he was the only grandchild that I known before the war. Today, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] he has a house full of grandchildren. He's a man with a white beard. But he's only-- he's only 48, 49 years old now. He's close to 50. But he-- he's-- he looks like an old man already. As you saw before-- picture before, my father was 47, he had the whole gray beard. And the man on the right-hand side, is my brother's brother-in-law. Unfortunately, he not-- he did not survive. He lived only another year after the war, after this picture was taken. And his wife is still alive. She remarried, started a new life. Also have children and grandchildren, great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was also a picture that we cherish very much, because we don't have these kind of pictures to be able to talk about. OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1947 in a city called Grosswardein in Romania. And when we left Romania, that's when I got a hold of this picture, and it's the only picture that I have. This is a picture of my brother and his wife. His name is Simcha Deutsch, And this picture was taken in 1948 after they were married in Bergen-Belsen. This is the brother that I told you at the beginning of the story, how he found me by the train. I wanted to leave and he arrived. And my wife was the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] for this sister-in-law of mine. She talked her into marrying my brother. We are, till this day, one of the best sisters-- sister-in-law's with the whole family. We are back and forth in each other's house, and we are very, very close. And thanks, to my wife, she should rest in peace. She brought them together. Now, he is same place where my wife is, unfortunately. And he raised family of four children. All four children married, and each of them have six, eight, nine children. And half of them are already married and have their own children. So he did raise, he actually, when he died, he left 31 grandchildren, and four great-grandchildren. And he was 70 years old when he passed away last year. He was a great man. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of me, Adolph Deutsch. This picture was taken in New York City, by the way, in 1949, when we knew that we are going to be-- have to become citizens. So we decided to have pictures prepared five years in advance. And as you can see, look at my hair, I had a full head of hair. And I was a handsome guy then, and we were in love, very much in love. Me and my wife, we had a wonderful time. We were in love till-- till the day she died. We-- we-- we were just like a bunch of teenagers. Everyday, we enjoyed every minute of our lives together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is me and my wife. It was taken [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] six years ago. We went to a banquet for the school where my kids used to go to. We attended their banquets every year. And this was like a souvenir for everyone that went to that banquet, because we were one of the originators that started that school here 38 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- yes, here in Los Angeles. And we were rewarded with a special frame because we were one of the first people that started that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us your wife's name again.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Lily Deutsch, and as you can see, she was full of life. She was very pleasant lady, and we enjoyed our life, every minute. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is me, [INAUDIBLE], with my two brothers, Meyer Isaac and Leizer Dov. It was taken in 1991. We went to-- one of my brother's grandson got married. And I don't know, my brother was already that time very sick. And so he couldn't come to take even a picture. We were lucky for him just to come to the wedding. And as you can see, we-- we were only three of us here, without my brother, the one that passed away last year. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is of my family, my daughter, my son-in-law, my three granddaughters. It was taken in 1990 in the backyard of their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is Mindy Weisel now. My son-in-law's name is Sheldon Weisel. The-- the girl with the big earring, her name is Caroline. The next one is [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Jessica. And the little girl below is Arianne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Washington, DC. That's where they live now. My daughter is an artist, specializing in Holocaust paintings. She had had shows all over the world. She has paintings sitting in Yad Vashem today. Anybody that goes to see Yad Vashem cannot miss to see. She has a portrait as big as a wall. And my son-in-law is an attorney in Washington, DC. And he works in one of the largest attorney's firm. They have 336 attorneys. Can imagine what it is there, what's going on. And my granddaughter, Caroline just graduated from-- from university, Boston University. And she is working as assistant to vice president in a publishing company in New York City. Jessica is ready to go to college, New York City. And Arianne just took an SAT test, one of the highest in the United States. There was not another one that came up for the test that she went to. And I got magazine that she was written up. And the school that she's going to is one of the top schools in the United States, and that is in Washington, DC. It's called the Charles E. Smith Day School. They have the finest school in the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture, as you see, is my son's family. His name is Jack Deutsch. And his family, Beverly Deutsch, and Canya Deutsch, Tamara Deutsch, Julia Deutsch, and Melanie Deutsch. There's also a fourth one, but he wasn't born then, because that was the time when my grandson became bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was done in 1990, that's four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son is a psychologist. He has his own office and is a clinical psychologist. He handles families and single, and he's a great doctor. My daughter-in-law is a lab technician, works in one of the hospitals in the valley. And my grandson is going to YULA, also just had the SAT test, had his grades just came back last Friday, 1,560 units. Also one of almost the highest. And, Tamara, now is 5'11'. She's only 13 years old. Also a straight A student. Julia is 11, straight A student. Melanie has been a little bit weak, till last year, now, she's in the second grade, and she is the second highest in her class. So I'm really proud of my family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As you see, this picture is one of my youngest grandchildren from my son, and it was taken in 1992. He was two years old, to his second birthday. That was the only birthday that my wife did not attend because unfortunately, she was very ill then already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Adam Deutsch. He speaks Spanish already. He's only four years old now. Speaks Spanish and Hebrew and English. And I'm really proud of him. He's a great kid. He loves to come to Zayde's house because Zayde has candies in the house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was taken here in North Hollywood at a second birthday, in my son's house.
